questions.question: According to the WHO, reproductive health means a total well-being in which aspects of reproduction?
questions.explanation: WHO defines reproductive health as total well-being in all aspects of reproduction, including physical, emotional, behavioural, and social aspects.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: In which year did India initiate the 'Family Planning' programmes at a national level?
questions.explanation: India was amongst the first in the world to initiate national family planning programmes in 1951.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: What is the current popular name for the improved programmes covering wider reproduction-related areas in India?
questions.explanation: The improved programmes are currently in operation under the popular name 'Reproductive and Child Health Care (RCH) programmes'.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Amniocentesis is a fetal sex determination test based on the ________ pattern in the amniotic fluid.
questions.explanation: Amniocentesis determines sex and genetic disorders based on the chromosomal pattern in the cells found in the amniotic fluid surrounding the fetus.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: 'Saheli', a new oral contraceptive for females, was developed by scientists at which institute?
questions.explanation: 'Saheli' was developed by the Central Drug Research Institute (CDRI) in Lucknow, India.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: According to the 2011 census report, the population growth rate in India was:
questions.explanation: According to the 2011 census, the population growth rate was still around 2% (20/1000/year), which is described as 'less than 2%'.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: What was the world population approximately in the year 2011?
questions.explanation: The world population was around 2 billion in 1900, 6 billion in 2000, and surged to 7.2 billion by 2011.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Which of the following is a 'Natural' method of contraception?
questions.explanation: Periodic abstinence is a natural method where couples avoid coitus from day 10 to 17 of the menstrual cycle.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: In periodic abstinence, couples avoid coitus during the 'fertile period', which is from day _____ of the menstrual cycle.
questions.explanation: Days 10 to 17 are considered the fertile period when chances of ovulation are high.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Lactational amenorrhea is effective as a contraceptive method only up to a maximum period of ________ months following parturition.
questions.explanation: Lactational amenorrhea (absence of menstruation) is effective only up to six months after childbirth.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Which barrier method is used to cover the cervix in the female reproductive tract?
questions.explanation: Diaphragms, cervical caps, and vaults are barriers made of rubber inserted into the female tract to cover the cervix.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Which of the following is a 'Non-medicated' IUD?
questions.explanation: Lippes loop is an example of a non-medicated IUD.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: How do Copper-releasing IUDs (like CuT, Cu7) prevent conception?
options: By suppressing sperm motility and fertilising capacity
questions.explanation: Cu ions released from these IUDs suppress sperm motility and the fertilising capacity of sperms.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Which of the following is a hormone-releasing IUD?
questions.explanation: Progestasert and LNG-20 are examples of hormone-releasing IUDs.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Oral contraceptive pills contain a combination of which hormones?
questions.explanation: Oral administration of small doses of either progestogens or progestogen-estrogen combinations is a popular contraceptive method.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Which of the following is TRUE about the oral pill 'Saheli'?
options: It is a 'once-a-week' pill with very few side effects
It has a high steroid concentration
questions.explanation: Saheli is a non-steroidal 'once-a-week' pill with very few side effects and high contraceptive value.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Emergency contraceptives must be administered within ________ hours of coitus to be effective.
questions.explanation: Progestogens or IUDs used within 72 hours of coitus are effective as emergency contraceptives.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: The surgical sterilization procedure in males is called:
questions.explanation: Vasectomy is the surgical method for sterilization in males where a small part of the vas deferens is removed or tied up.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: In tubectomy, which part of the female reproductive system is removed or tied up?
questions.explanation: Tubectomy involves removing or tying up a small part of the fallopian tube.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Medical Termination of Pregnancy (MTP) was legalised by the Government of India in which year?
questions.explanation: Government of India legalised MTP in 1971 with strict conditions to avoid misuse.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: MTP is considered relatively safe only during which trimester of pregnancy?
questions.explanation: MTPs are considered relatively safe during the first trimester, i.e., up to 12 weeks of pregnancy.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a Sexually Transmitted Disease (STD)?
questions.explanation: Gonorrhoea, syphilis, and genital herpes are STDs. Encephalitis is not listed as an STD in the text.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Which of the following STDs are NOT completely curable even if detected early?
options: Hepatitis-B, Genital herpes and HIV infection
questions.explanation: Except for hepatitis-B, genital herpes, and HIV infections, other STDs are completely curable if detected early.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Hepatitis-B and HIV can also be transmitted by:
questions.explanation: Besides sexual contact, these diseases are transmitted through needles, blood transfusion, and from mother to fetus.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Infertility is defined as the inability to produce children even after ________ of unprotected sexual co-habitation.
questions.explanation: Infertility is the inability to conceive or produce children even after 2 years of unprotected sexual co-habitation.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: In 'In Vitro Fertilisation' (IVF), where does the fertilisation occur?
options: Outside the body in simulated conditions
questions.explanation: In IVF, fertilisation takes place outside the body in conditions similar to those in the body.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: ZIFT stands for:
questions.explanation: ZIFT stands for Zygote Intra Fallopian Transfer.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: If an embryo with more than 8 blastomeres is transferred, it is usually transferred into the:
questions.explanation: Embryos with more than 8 blastomeres are transferred into the uterus (IUT – Intra Uterine Transfer).
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: GIFT (Gamete Intra Fallopian Transfer) is a technique used for females who:
options: Cannot provide suitable environment for fertilisation
questions.explanation: GIFT involves transferring an ovum from a donor into the fallopian tube of another female who cannot produce one.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: ICSI stands for:
questions.explanation: ICSI is Intra Cytoplasmic Sperm Injection, where a sperm is directly injected into the ovum.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Artificial Insemination (AI) is useful in cases where:
options: The female cannot produce an egg
The male is unable to inseminate the female or has very low sperm count
questions.explanation: AI is used when the male partner cannot inseminate or has a low sperm count (oligospermia).
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: What is the primary motive of the statutory ban on amniocentesis for sex determination in India?
questions.explanation: The ban is intended to curb female foeticide, which is prevalent due to a preference for male children.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT an ideal characteristic of a contraceptive?
questions.explanation: An ideal contraceptive should be reversible. Irreversibility is not a desired trait.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: The 'Withdrawal' or 'Coitus Interruptus' method involves:
options: Withdrawing the penis from the vagina just before ejaculation
questions.explanation: This method involves the male partner withdrawing just before ejaculation to avoid insemination.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Condoms are barriers made of thin rubber/latex sheath used to cover the:
options: Vagina or cervix in the female
questions.explanation: Condoms are available for both males (covering the penis) and females (covering the vagina/cervix).
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: What additional benefit do condoms provide besides contraception?
questions.explanation: Use of condoms protects the user from contracting STDs and AIDS.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: What is the most widely accepted method of contraception in India currently?
questions.explanation: IUDs are one of the most widely accepted methods of contraception in India.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Progestogens inhibit pregnancy by:
options: Altering cervical mucus quality to retard sperm entry
questions.explanation: Pills/progestogens inhibit ovulation/implantation and alter cervical mucus to prevent sperm entry.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Subcutaneous 'Implants' used by females contain:
options: Progestogens alone or in combination with estrogen
questions.explanation: Implants under the skin contain progestogens similar to pills.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Sterilization methods are generally advised as a ________ option.
questions.explanation: Surgical methods (vasectomy/tubectomy) are terminal methods to prevent any more pregnancies.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Approximately how many MTPs are performed in a year all over the world?
questions.explanation: Nearly 45 to 50 million MTPs are performed in a year world-wide.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Why is MTP performed?
options: To get rid of unwanted pregnancy
When pregnancy is harmful to the mother or fetus
To choose the sex of the child
questions.explanation: MTP is for unwanted pregnancies (failure of contraceptive/rape) or when the pregnancy poses a risk to the mother or child.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: The incidence of STDs is reported to be very high among persons in the age group of:
questions.explanation: Incidence of STDs is reported to be very high among the 15-24 age group.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a complication of untreated STDs?
questions.explanation: STDs can cause PID, abortions, still births, ectopic pregnancies, and infertility. Diabetes is not a complication of STDs.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: One of the best ways to prevent STDs is to:
options: Avoid coitus with unknown partners/multiple partners
Consult a doctor for early detection
questions.explanation: Prevention includes avoiding multiple partners, using condoms, and seeking medical help for suspected symptoms.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: A 'test tube baby' refers to a baby born through:
options: In Vitro Fertilisation followed by embryo transfer
Complete development in a glass tube
questions.explanation: Test tube baby programme involves IVF followed by ET (Embryo Transfer).
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: In 'Intra-uterine Insemination' (IUI), the semen is introduced into the:
questions.explanation: In IUI, the semen (from husband/donor) is artificially introduced into the uterus.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Vaginal diaphragms are ________ contraceptives.
questions.explanation: Diaphragms are barrier methods used by females.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: In the 'Lactational Amenorrhea' method, which of the following happens?
options: High levels of hormones prevent conception naturally
questions.explanation: Intense lactation inhibits ovulation and therefore the cycle, providing a natural contraceptive effect.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Which of the following describes 'Ammensalism' correctly?
questions.explanation: Ammensalism is an interaction where one species is harmed and the other is unaffected.
questions.subject: Biology
